title: RaDE Rabies Exposure Decision-Support Workflow for DHIS2
description: WHO-aligned, decision-tree-ready rabies PEP intake checklist for the RaDE DHIS2 app competition. This version preserves the RaDE-compatible JSON shape while adding structured country-level geography, an embedded UKHSA terrestrial mammal rabies risk-by-country catalog, optional future-ready subnational detail, an explicit bat lyssavirus special-context derived field, and a revised global rabies-relevant animal taxonomy. The animal taxonomy distinguishes owned/stray/feral/unknown dogs and cats, separates domestic ferrets from wild mustelids, separates vampire bats from other bats, and adds globally relevant carnivore, livestock, herbivore, low-risk mammal, captive/exotic, and non-mammal groupings. The taxonomy is a rabies-risk and public-health-management taxonomy, not a complete mammalian taxonomy.

sources.title: WHO SEARO Rabies Post-Exposure Prophylaxis Decision Tree
RaDE operational/context fields and global epidemiology taxonomy retained for demo usability
UKHSA terrestrial mammal country-risk overlay, with separate bat lyssavirus context

validation.intended_use: DHIS2 app competition demo; WHO-defensible decision support; UKHSA country-level terrestrial mammal rabies risk output with future subnational and animal-specific overlay support
validation.version_change_summary: Creates rabies_intake_v2 from rabies_intake_v1; preserves the UKHSA terrestrial mammal country risk overlay; adds an explicit bat lyssavirus special-context derived field, including Australia/Australian bat lyssavirus handling; refines selected global animal taxonomy labels for camelids, herpestids, large rodents, marine mammals, and megafauna.

sections.title: Animal clinical features and risk assessment

questions.text: What was the date and time of the exposure?
questions.rationale: Exact exposure date/time is essential for determining PEP urgency, observation windows, and whether thresholds such as the WHO 10-day rule apply. Both source checklists reference timing indirectly but neither captures the actual date.
inline_notes: Used to determine whether the exposure occurred more than 10 days ago for the WHO decision tree.

questions.text: What free-text geographic details are known about where the exposure occurred?
questions.rationale: Free-text location detail supports audit, local follow-up, and later country/subnational mapping. Structured epidemiology logic is captured in the country/territory field, with subnational detail retained for future refinement.
inline_notes: Use this for city, district, landmark, travel itinerary, or narrative location details; automated epidemiology logic uses the structured country/territory field.

questions.text: What country or territory did the exposure occur in?

options.label: South Georgia and the South Sandwich Islands
Spain – mainland, Balearic and Canary Islands
Spain – north African territories of Ceuta and Melilla
Wake Island and the US Pacific Islands

questions.rationale: Country or territory is used as the national terrestrial mammal rabies epidemiology overlay and maps directly to the embedded UKHSA rabies risk-by-country catalog.
inline_notes: Used to derive the UKHSA country-level rabies risk for terrestrial mammals. Bat lyssavirus risk is not derived from this country risk level and should be handled through bat exposure guidance and local public health review.
Australia is a special bat lyssavirus context: classical terrestrial rabies is absent, but Australian bat lyssavirus is relevant for bat exposures.
Subnational detail may refine this in future versions but does not currently override the national catalog.

questions.text: What subnational area did the exposure occur in, if known?
questions.rationale: Subnational detail is retained for audit and future local terrestrial mammal rabies ecology overlays, including animal-specific or province/state/district risk stratification.
inline_notes: Optional for the DHIS2 competition demo; retained for future subnational and animal-specific overlays and not used to override the current UKHSA national terrestrial mammal risk output.

questions.text: Was the animal a mammal?
inline_notes: If no, species is not susceptible to rabies: no PEP indicated; wash exposed areas with soap and water.

questions.text: What type of animal was involved?

options.label: Dog - ownership or roaming status unknown
Cat - ownership or roaming status unknown
Wild canid - fox, jackal, wolf, coyote, African wild dog, dhole, etc.
Raccoon or other procyonid (e.g., coati, kinkajou, olingo)
Other mustelid (e.g., badger, weasel, otter, mink, marten, fisher, wolverine)
Wild felid (e.g., bobcat, lynx, cougar, leopard, lion, tiger)
Livestock - ruminant or camelid (e.g., cattle, buffalo, sheep, goat, camel, llama, alpaca)
Horse, donkey, mule, or other equid
Pig, wild boar, warthog, or other suid
Wild ungulate, large herbivore, or megafauna (e.g., deer, antelope, elk, moose, kudu, elephant)
Large rodent (e.g., groundhog/woodchuck, beaver, muskrat, porcupine, capybara, other large hystricomorph rodent)
Small rodent (e.g., mouse, rat, squirrel, chipmunk, hamster, guinea pig, vole)
Shrew, mole, hedgehog, or similar small insectivore
Marine mammal (e.g., seal, sea lion, walrus, dolphin, whale)
Captive, zoo, laboratory, or exotic mammal

questions.source_phrase: What type of animal was involved?
inline_notes: Used to derive mammal/rodent/bat/domestic-animal/wildlife flags, terrestrial mammal country-risk interpretation, and bat lyssavirus special-context flags without changing the WHO wound-category logic.
Select the closest functional group. If exact species or local/common name is known, capture it in the species-detail field.
This field is not intended to be a complete animal taxonomy; it is a rabies-risk and public-health-management taxonomy.
questions.rationale: Global animal group taxonomy supports rabies exposure review and future animal-specific overlays while retaining a concise intake UI. Categories are functional rabies-risk groups rather than a complete mammalian taxonomy. Dogs and cats are subdivided by ownership/roaming status because this affects vaccination likelihood, animal availability, observation feasibility, and public health management. Domestic ferrets, raccoon dogs, ferret-badgers, vampire bats, mephitids, procyonids, and selected wild carnivore groups are separated because they have distinct regional rabies relevance or operational handling.

questions.text: If known, what was the exact species or local/common animal name?
inline_notes: Use local/common name if exact species is uncertain.
This field supports public health review and future animal-specific, country, and subnational catalog refinement.
questions.rationale: The structured animal group supports decision logic, while free-text species detail preserves local specificity without overloading the intake UI.

questions.text: Was the animal a rodent?
inline_notes: WHO decision-tree branch: rodents rarely transmit rabies. For operational overlays, distinguish small rodents from larger rodents such as groundhogs/woodchucks, beavers, porcupines, or capybaras when possible.

questions.text: Did the exposure occur more than 10 days ago?
questions.source_phrase: Did the exposure occur >10 days ago?
inline_notes: For dogs, cats, and domestic ferrets, healthy 10 days after exposure presents no rabies risk. Other animals may require a more conservative 14-day clinical investigation per WHO footnote 1.

questions.text: Is the animal still alive?
inline_notes: If exposure was more than 10 days ago and the animal is still alive, PEP is not indicated or can be discontinued, subject to animal species and local policy.

questions.text: Is the victim less than 14 years old?
Does the victim have multiple or deep wounds?
questions.source_phrase: Does the victim have multiple or deep wounds?

questions.text: Does the victim have wounds in highly innervated areas?
questions.source_phrase: Does the victim have wounds in highly innervated areas?
inline_notes: Highly innervated areas include head, neck, face, genitals, and hands. A yes response triggers start PEP immediately in the WHO decision tree.

questions.text: What is the anatomical site of the wound or exposure?
questions.rationale: Anatomical site determines innervation density (head, neck, face, genitals, hands are high-risk) and guides local RIG infiltration. WHO footnote 2 and q07 reference highly innervated areas; capturing the specific site enables automated high-risk flagging.
inline_notes: Optional structured detail to support the WHO high-innervation branch and RIG infiltration planning.

questions.text: Did the animal show signs of disease consistent with rabies?

source_map.who_text: Did the animal show any signs of disease consistent with rabies?
source_map.who_source_phrase: Did the animal show any signs of disease consistent with rabies?
source_map.on_text: Did the animal display behaviour compatible with rabies?

questions.text: Did the animal die or disappear within 10 days of the exposure?
questions.source_phrase: Did the animal die or disappear within 10 days of the exposure?

questions.text: Did the animal bite 2 or more people or animals?
questions.source_phrase: Did the animal bite 2 or more people or animals?

questions.text: Has the animal been tested for rabies by a WOAH-recommended test?
questions.source_phrase: Has the animal been tested for rabies by a WOAH recommended test?

questions.text: What is the animal rabies test result?
questions.source_phrase: Test positive / Test pending / inconclusive / Test negative

questions.text: Is the animal available for investigation, quarantine, or rabies testing?

source_map.who_text: Is the animal available for investigation and quarantine?
source_map.who_source_phrase: Is the animal available for investigation and quarantine?
source_map.on_text: Can the animal be identified and located for observation or rabies testing?
source_map.on_source_phrase: Can the animal be identified and located for observation or rabies testing?

questions.text: Is the animal up-to-date on vaccination?
questions.source_phrase: Is the animal up-to-date on vaccination?

questions.text: Was the animal exposed to rabies risk in the last 12 months?
questions.source_phrase: Was the animal exposed to rabies risk in the last 12 months?

questions.text: Was the animal provoked to bite or scratch?
questions.source_phrase: Was the animal provoked to bite /scratch?

questions.text: Which WHO wound/exposure category best describes the exposure?

options.label: Category I: animal licks on intact skin, touching or feeding the animal
Category II: minor scratches or abrasions, nibbling of uncovered skin; skin broken but wound not bleeding
Category III: transdermal bites or scratches, licks on broken skin, mucous membrane contamination with saliva, or exposure to a bat

questions.source_phrase: Skin is intact / Skin is broken but wound not bleeding / Skin is broken and wound bleeding OR mucous membrane contaminated with saliva
inline_notes: Category I: no PEP indicated, wash exposed areas. Category II: wound washing and ARV unless recent completed PEP pathway applies. Category III: wound washing, RIG/RmAbs, and ARV immediately unless prior vaccination/recent PEP pathway applies. If exposure was to a bat, treat as WHO category III.

questions.text: Which specific exposure mechanisms occurred?

items.label: Bite that penetrated skin or caused bleeding
Bite that did NOT penetrate skin or cause bleeding
Scratch that broke skin or caused bleeding
Scratch that did NOT break skin or cause bleeding
Neural tissue contacting broken skin or mucous membrane

questions.source_phrase: Did ANY of the following occur? - Bite / Scratch that broke skin / Saliva contacting broken skin / Saliva contacting mucosa / Neural tissue contacting broken skin or mucosa
inline_notes: Optional structured detail to support or audit the WHO wound-category selection.

questions.text: Has thorough wound washing with soap and water and antiseptic application been performed?
questions.rationale: WHO footnote 5 states wound washing is 'a life-saving practice especially for immunocompromised patients' and recommends 15 minutes of washing. ON admin notes describe it as 'imperative and probably the most effective procedure in the prevention of rabies'. Neither checklist captures whether this critical first-aid step has been performed.

questions.text: Is the patient immunocompromised?

source_map.on_text: Is the patient currently immunocompromised or on chloroquine/hydroxychloroquine?
source_map.on_source_phrase: Is the patient currently immunocompromised or on chloroquine/hydroxychloroquine?

inline_notes: In the WHO decision tree, immunocompromise escalates Category II and Category III pathways toward RIG/RmAbs plus ARV, with serology where feasible.

questions.text: Has the patient ever received any rabies vaccination?

source_map.who_text: Has the patient ever received any rabies vaccination?
source_map.who_source_phrase: Patient: ever received any rabies vaccination?
source_map.on_text: Has the patient previously received any rabies vaccine?
source_map.on_source_phrase: Has the patient previously received rabies vaccine?

questions.text: Has the patient completed PEP in the past 3 months?

source_map.who_text: Has the patient completed rabies PEP less than 3 months ago?
source_map.who_source_phrase: Patient: completed PEP less than 3 months ago?
source_map.on_text: Did the patient complete an RPEP series in the past 3 months?
source_map.on_source_phrase: Did the patient complete an RPEP series in the past 3 months?

questions.text: Is RIG/RmAbs availability limited?
Is the patient high-risk according to WHO footnote 9?
inline_notes: High-risk includes multiple bites, deep wounds, bites to highly innervated body parts, severe immunodeficiency, bites from an animal with probable or confirmed rabies, and exposure to a bat. In RIG/RmAbs-limited settings, high-risk status prioritizes RIG/RmAbs with ARV.

derived_fields.name: ukhsa_country_terrestrial_mammal_rabies_risk_level
derived_fields.description: Derived UKHSA terrestrial mammal rabies risk level from the selected country or territory. This derived field excludes bats and related bat lyssaviruses.

derived_fields.name: rabies_epidemiology_note
derived_fields.description: Plain-language note explaining the UKHSA country-level terrestrial mammal rabies risk output, any terrestrial animal-specific exception, and that bat lyssavirus risk and subnational detail require separate handling.

derived_fields.name: geography_resolution_used
derived_fields.description: Resolution level used to assign the current terrestrial mammal rabies risk output. Current automated output uses country_or_territory; subnational area is retained for future overlays.

derived_fields.name: dog_cat_domesticity_status
derived_fields.description: Derived status for dog/cat exposures to support risk explanation and local policy overlays.

derived_fields.name: animal_functional_group
derived_fields.description: Normalized functional group for downstream risk mapping, derived from the structured animal group and optional species/local-name detail.

derived_fields.name: bat_lyssavirus_special_context
derived_fields.description: Derived flag for bat lyssavirus contexts that are not represented by the UKHSA terrestrial mammal country risk output. Australia should derive australia_or_bat_lyssavirus_region for bat exposures because Australian bat lyssavirus is relevant despite no classical terrestrial rabies risk. Other bat exposures should remain outside the terrestrial mammal country-risk output and be handled through bat-specific guidance and local public health review.

ukhsa_terrestrial_mammal_rabies_country_.description: Embedded UKHSA rabies risk-by-country catalog for national terrestrial mammal rabies epidemiology output. The UKHSA country risk levels describe terrestrial animals only: no-risk means no indigenous rabies in terrestrial animals; low-risk means rabies occurs in wild animals but not companion animals; high-risk means rabies occurs in wild and companion animals or there are no data to prove otherwise. Related lyssaviruses in bats are outside this country risk output and require separate bat-exposure handling. Australia is explicitly identified as a bat lyssavirus special context because Australian bat lyssavirus is relevant despite no classical terrestrial rabies risk.

special_notes: Country/territory values are taken from the supplied UKHSA risk-level list and include territories and special geographic entries where listed.
The current automated epidemiology output is national/country-level for terrestrial mammals only.

Bats and related lyssaviruses are not classified by this terrestrial mammal country risk output. Bat exposures should be handled through the bat-specific animal/exposure pathway and local public health guidance.
Australia is explicitly surfaced as a bat lyssavirus special context: classical terrestrial rabies is absent, but Australian bat lyssavirus is relevant for bat exposures.
Subnational area is retained as an intake field for future local overlays and audit but does not currently override the UKHSA national terrestrial mammal risk value.
Terrestrial animal-specific exception text is retained where UKHSA lists wildlife exceptions, such as foxes, skunks, or racoons.
United Kingdom imported animal exposure is represented as a separate listed option because the supplied UKHSA table maps it to Contact RIgS.

risk_level_definitions.no_risk: No indigenous rabies in terrestrial animals. Related lyssaviruses in bats are separate from this country risk level.
risk_level_definitions.low_risk: Rabies occurs in wild terrestrial animals but not in companion animals. Related lyssaviruses in bats are separate from this country risk level.
risk_level_definitions.high_risk: Rabies occurs in wild and companion terrestrial animals, or there are no data to prove otherwise. Related lyssaviruses in bats are separate from this country risk level.

australia.label: Australia
australia.rationale: Classical terrestrial rabies is absent, but Australian bat lyssavirus is relevant for bat exposures.

external_catalogs.purpose: Maps selected country or territory to the UKHSA terrestrial mammal rabies risk level and normalized risk code. Bat lyssavirus risk is excluded and handled separately, with Australia explicitly surfaced as a special bat lyssavirus context.
